# Manufacturing Process Copilot
## Document 02 — Final Dataset Schema

**Status:** Production-Ready  
**Version:** 1.0  
**Generated by:** `ml/scripts/generate_synthetic_data.py`

---

## 1. Dataset Overview

| Property | Value |
|---|---|
| Format | CSV (comma-separated, UTF-8) |
| Total columns | 50 |
| ML feature columns | 37 |
| Target columns | 4 |
| Metadata/identifier columns | 9 |
| 120-day validation size | 954 rows |
| Projected 540-day full size | ~4,293 rows |
| Missing values | 0 (guaranteed by construction) |
| Temporal order | Chronological by planned_start |

---

## 2. Target Columns

These are the prediction targets. They are placed at the end of the CSV. They must never be used as input features in any ML pipeline.

### `is_delayed` — Primary classification target

| Property | Value |
|---|---|
| Type | int64 |
| Values | 0 = on time, 1 = delayed |
| Base rate | 36.6% positive (120-day run) |
| Definition | `actual_end > planned_end` (strict inequality, one-minute resolution) |

The positive rate reflects realistic SME manufacturing conditions. It is intentionally higher than the theoretical minimum to provide a balanced training signal. Class imbalance is mild enough (37/63 split) that no oversampling is required, though class-weight adjustment is recommended.

---

### `delay_minutes` — Primary regression target

| Property | Value |
|---|---|
| Type | int64 |
|  Unit | Minutes |
| Range | 0 (on-time orders) to 7,590 |
| Mean (delayed orders only) | 644 minutes |
| Median (delayed orders only) | 266 minutes |
| P95 (delayed orders only) | 2,390 minutes |
| Distribution shape | Right-skewed log-normal; apply log1p transform before regression |

Zero values indicate on-time completion. Models trained on this target should use the delayed-orders subset only, or frame it as a zero-inflated regression problem.

---

### `delay_category` — Ordinal classification target

| Property | Value |
|---|---|
| Type | str (categorical) |
| Cardinality | 5 classes |
| Encoding | Ordinal (natural ordering) |

| Category | Threshold | Minutes range | Count (120-day) | Share |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| on_time | 0 minutes | 0 | 608 | 63.7% |
| minor_delay | 1–60 minutes | 1–60 | 65 | 6.8% |
| moderate_delay | 61–480 minutes | 61–478 | 148 | 15.5% |
| major_delay | 481–1440 minutes | 481–1,390 | 87 | 9.1% |
| critical_delay | > 1440 minutes | 1,441–7,590 | 46 | 4.8% |

Use label encoding 0–4 in the order shown. For ordinal regression, an appropriate loss function (e.g. cumulative logit) should be used.

---

### `delay_root_cause` — Multi-class classification target

| Property | Value |
|---|---|
| Type | str (categorical) |
| Cardinality | 7 classes |
| Task | Multi-class classification |

| Class | Description | Share (delayed orders) |
|---|---|---|
| `none` | No discrete event; delay from scheduling/processing variance | 19.5% |
| `setup_overrun` | Actual setup exceeded 1.7× standard | 25.5% |
| `material_unavailability` | Component shortage caused hold | 18.1% |
| `machine_breakdown` | Unplanned machine failure during order | 18.1% |
| `multiple_causes` | Two or more causes fired in same order | 8.9% |
| `planning_schedule_conflict` | Queue wait / shift boundary conflict | 8.3% |
| `quality_failure_rework` | First-pass inspection failure | 1.7% |

Root cause is assigned only from events that actually occurred in the simulation — not sampled independently. `none` does not mean the delay was unexplained; it means the delay emerged from accumulated processing variance without any single discrete triggering event.

---

## 3. ML Feature Columns

Full specifications are in Document 03 (Feature Dictionary). Summary by category:

| Category | Column count | Prefix/description |
|---|---|---|
| Order planning | 8 | Lead time, tightness, priority, expedite, revision, estimated hours |
| Product characteristics | 3 | Complexity, BOM, ops count |
| Machine state at release | 8 | Utilisation, OEE, downtime, maintenance, changeover, queue depth |
| Operator state at release | 5 | Tier, experience, load, shift position |
| Material state at release | 2 | Availability flag, shortage count |
| Historical rolling (90d/30d) | 7 | Product/machine/operator delay rates, FPY, setup overrun |
| Temporal | 4 | DOW, hour, month-end flag, quarter-end flag |
| **Total** | **37** | |

---

## 4. Identifier and Metadata Columns

These columns are for lineage, debugging, and post-hoc analysis. They must be dropped before ML model training.

| Column | Type | Description |
|---|---|---|
| `order_id` | str | Unique order identifier. Format: `ORD-000001` |
| `po_number` | str | Purchase order number. Format: `PO-00001` |
| `product_id` | str | Simulated product. Format: `PROD-001` through `PROD-015` |
| `machine_id` | str | Assigned machine. Format: `MACH-001` through `MACH-008` |
| `operator_id` | str | Assigned operator. Format: `OPR-001` through `OPR-024` |
| `priority` | str | Human-readable priority: `critical`, `high`, `normal`, `low` |
| `shift_type` | str | Shift name: `morning`, `afternoon`, `night` |
| `planned_start` | str (ISO 8601) | When manufacturing was scheduled to begin |
| `actual_end` | str (ISO 8601) | When manufacturing actually completed |

`priority` and `shift_type` are the string versions of `priority_encoded` and `shift_type_encoded`. Do not use both; use the encoded version for ML.

---

## 6. Encoding Reference

### `priority_encoded`
| Value | Label |
|---|---|
| 1 | normal |
| 2 | high |
| 3 | critical |

Note: `low` priority orders are present in the simulation but rare; they may map to value 0 if they appear. In the 120-day validation run, ~57% normal, ~33% high, ~10% critical.

### `shift_type_encoded`
| Value | Label |
|---|---|
| 0 | morning (06:00–14:00) |
| 1 | afternoon (14:00–22:00) |
| 2 | night (22:00–06:00) |

### `operator_skill_tier_encoded`
| Value | Label |
|---|---|
| 0.0 | junior (1–12 months experience) |
| 1.0 | mid (13–48 months experience) |
| 2.0 | senior (49–180 months experience) |

### Boolean fields (`int64`, 0 or 1)
`is_delayed`, `is_expedited`, `is_month_end`, `is_quarter_end`, `material_availability_at_release`, `maintenance_due_within_order_window`, `changeover_required`

---

## 7. Train/Val/Test Split Protocol

Splits are time-ordered and non-overlapping. The split is applied on `planned_start` timestamp, sorted ascending.

```
|<────── train (71%) ──────>|<─ val (18%) ─>|<─ test (11%) ─>|
     oldest orders                               newest orders
```

No random shuffling occurs before splitting. This simulates the deployment scenario: the model is trained on past data and evaluated on future data. Any feature that encodes future information (e.g. `product_delay_rate_90d` computed using future orders) would be caught by performance degradation on the test split.

Leakage checks in the feature engineering pipeline must confirm that all rolling-window features use only `release_time - window_days` as their lookback, never forward.

---

## 8. Known Data Characteristics

These properties should inform preprocessing and model selection decisions.

**Right-skewed continuous features.** `planned_lead_time_hours`, `release_lag_hours`, `estimated_total_hours`, `quantity`, `operator_experience_months`, `machine_unplanned_downtime_hours_30d` are all right-skewed. Apply log1p transform in the sklearn Pipeline.

**Bimodal utilisation.** `machine_utilization_at_release` is bimodal (~50% near 0.0, ~43% near 1.0). This reflects early simulation periods (no history → 0 utilisation) versus steady-state. The model should handle this without special treatment; tree-based models are robust to it.

**Sparse boolean features.** `is_expedited` (3.1%), `maintenance_due_within_order_window` (1.4%), `schedule_revision_count` (3.1%), `is_quarter_end` (9.6%) are infrequent positives. No imputation needed (values are structural zeros, not missing).

**Strong predictors.** `work_center_queue_depth_at_release` (|r|=0.50) and `release_lag_hours` (|r|=0.48) are the strongest linear predictors of delay. `material_availability_at_release` has the strongest causal signal (69.3% vs 32.1% delay rate).

**Correlated historical features.** `product_delay_rate_90d` and `product_x_machine_delay_rate_90d` (|r|≈0.94 with each other) are high correlated. Keep both — the interaction term captures machine-specific product variance that the margin rate misses. A VIF analysis is recommended in EDA.

---
